Okay, Susan, you got me. Unless someone else joins, I'm all yours. What were you saying? So what I was thinking about is what are stakes and how do you create them in a story? Got it. So are we talking business or personal? Personal, but also, and also are stakes needed for every scene or yeah, basically, that's those are my questions. Yeah, that's a good question. Well, first of all, they're not needed for every scene. Some stories, although they're not common, only have sort of one stake, one thing is at stake. Ideally, hi, Kat, ideally, stories have more than one stake and we wanna deploy them strategically throughout a story. Basically, a stake is it's anything in a story where you want something, you need something, you're trying to avoid something, you're afraid of something, you're trying to defeat the bad guy, win the race. It's the thing that we're supposed to sort of be rooting for the protagonist or sometimes in many of my stories, I create stories where the audience ends up rooting against me because I am telling a story about a bad thing that I did. And I get the audience to actually wish for my comeuppance, which is fine too. So essentially at the beginning of every story, you should be aiming at something at the end of the story. You should be wanting something, needing something, desiring something, looking to avoid something. And if we get, that's the big stake that we're sort of aiming at as we get through the story, but oftentimes we can deploy some smaller stakes along the way. Does that make sense? Yes, it does. I wish I have, I'm actually going to a story slam to tomorrow night in Boston, but I have not begun working on that story. Otherwise I would say, oh, here's a great example of a stake. But I guess a story I recently told was about my, it's gonna be actually on the, it's gonna be on a podcast this week. It was a story about my daughter constantly talking to people and making me crazy about it. She's always seeking to speak to other people. She talks to moms about their new baby. She talks to kids about whatever she can. She's constantly trying to connect to people. And this was at a time before I knew she had autism, which makes what she was doing even more remarkable. And so the stake in the story, the initial stake is, I'm just trying to get my luggage from the baggage carousel and get the hell out of the airport with my family who are making me crazy. And my daughter is making me crazy in particular, she's going to talk to some 11 year old girl and my daughter is about 11 at the time too. And it's just, it begins as a story about a beleaguered husband who's being made crazy by his family who do not travel lightly or efficiently in any way whatsoever and how awful it is to have to travel with these monsters. And so the stake in the story is, will Matt get out of the airport? Will he get out quickly? Will he get out without killing anyone? Will he get out without upsetting his wife and children? All of those things. And then eventually the story actually becomes a stake about me needing something, me needing to understand my daughter because someone comes up to me, a man actually approaches me as we're leaving the airport and stops me and I think he's like mad at me. He says, your daughter was just talking to my daughter. And I think, oh no, what did Clara say to that girl? And he tells me, he says, your daughter's remarkable. He says, it's amazing how your daughter can walk up to a stranger and engage in a conversation. And it was a beautiful conversation. He said, my daughter's chronically shy and could never do that. And I hope that someday my daughter can be like yours. And suddenly I'm confronted with the idea that it's making me crazy what she does. And in the eyes of someone better than me, it's viewed as something wonderful. So the stake becomes, I need to see my daughter through better eyes. That's the final landing place of that story. But along the way I plant these other stakes like, how long will that take for the baggage to come out? Will my daughter like make me crazy? Will my son stop licking things long enough to get him out without catching a disease? Those kinds of things, does that make sense? Yeah, yes it does. I think the problem sometimes people have in deploying stakes in a story is either they throw them all right in front, right at the top of the story, because they wanna grab everyone's attention and sort of like ensure that people are listening. And what that really does is it ensures that they will stop listening eventually because there's nothing else to sort of raise the stakes. And the other problem people have is they'll give away the ultimate stake of a story in the beginning by saying something like, this is a story about a time when I needed to understand my daughter in a way I never had before. Which essentially gives away the stake at the end of the story that the surprising moment of understanding that I need to have at the end, it steals all the surprise and sort of lays out that stake right off the top instead of allowing it to play out in land where it's supposed to land. So we wanna spread them out if we have more than one. Sometimes we don't have more than one. Sometimes it's just I need to get from A to B and that's the stake. Can he get from A to B in the amount of time he has to do it? Yeah, but they're important. They're necessary in all stories. A good way to start looking at stakes is and is to just pay attention to movies that you're watching and ask yourself what's the stake in the story. What's the ultimate stake? Which is usually kind of identifiable because movies tend to have genres and genres and tend to define the stakes. If you're watching Mission Impossible, Tom Cruise must complete the impossible mission. If you're watching a romantic comedy, the two people in the beginning who don't love each other must love each other at the end. So those stakes are sort of kind of evident at the top of movies because of the genre, but you can watch these stakes get elevated along the way. If it's Mission Impossible, it's a series of missions that lead Tom Cruise to the ultimate mission. If it's a romantic comedy, it is, will the first date go well? Will the second date go well? Will the misunderstanding be resolved? How will it be resolved? How quickly will it be resolved? Will the in-laws like the guy? Will the in-laws hate the guy? Those are stakes that lead us eventually to a first kiss or a wedding or whatever it happens to be. So if you pay attention to the way movies operate in that way, I often think that it can be instructive to us as storytellers in terms of stretching out stakes, deploying them, raising stakes, things like that. Yeah. That makes sense? Yes, it does. It's a little trickier in true storytelling because in fiction, when I'm writing my novels, if I suddenly feel like we need to raise the stakes, I can just create a stake. I can insert a new problem that my character must now face and I can continue to escalate problems along the way because I get to make everything up. In our life, we have to identify the stakes as best we can and whenever we can, space them out, heighten the sense of them, make them really clear and obvious to the audience, those kinds of things. Because we don't get to, at least I don't make up anything that never happened to me in a story. So I'm stuck with the truth and I have to make the truth work for me. Yeah. So how would stakes and stories change and you asked whether the story was business or personal? How would that change if it were a business story? Well, in a business story, stakes tend to be a little more defined. They tend to be, if you're selling a product or a service, let's say, the stakes tend to be, why do you need to buy this product? Why do you need to subscribe to this service? Why is my product better than my competitor's product? Why is my company worthy of investment as opposed to the company down the street from me? So a lot of times the stakes in a business story tend to be a clear indication that we are selling something, we want you to believe in something and the stakes are, will you believe in it? Or can we prove to you that it is the best choice or the least costly choice or the most reliable choice, those kinds of things? We can still play stakes in business stories, though, like if it's a tire company, part of the stakes are you might open your marketing deck or your advertisement or your commercial with a flat tire and the stake is it's 2 a.m. and it's raining and you get a flat tire and let's say it's a young mom with three small children, right? That raises the stakes. Because we can manipulate things in an advertisement, it doesn't have to be true, we might create that as a stake at the beginning of our marketing pitches. 26-year-old mom on a highway late at night in the rain gets a flat tire, three little kids in the back, what does she do, right? That's a problem and the problem really is you bought the wrong tire. Why did you buy the wrong tire? Well, let me tell you why. Well, let me explain to you why my tire is the tire you should buy. So you open with a stake of, oof, if you buy the wrong tire, disaster strikes and then the stake shifts to why my tire is better than their tire and then maybe the stake shifts to why is my tire more economically efficient or more reliable or our customer service and our AAA sort of service that comes out and takes care of you right away without having to get out of the car or wait very long. All of those things become added stakes. Like ours is better than theirs, ours is better than theirs, that kind of a thing. It's like a competition. So it's similar, it's less nuanced, it's a little more blunt force. People know what to expect in terms of those stakes, but they can be really subtle too. Steve Jobs opens his iPhone announcement in 2007 by saying, I have been waiting almost two years to take this stage and talk to you about this new Apple launch. That's a stake. That is, wow, he's been waiting two years to tell us this, right? That establishes a stake. It also creates suspense, right? Cause we're wondering what could possibly take two years to create, but the stake is it better be good. Like there's something good coming because if this took this guy two years to do it, something serious is at stake here. So sometimes it can be more subtle, but a lot of times they're just a little more obvious in business, but it's the same thing. You want people to get to believe in you, to buy into what you're doing, to trust that what your company is doing is better than the other company, and that's the stake that is presented. But I always say every story has suspense and surprise, and every story has to have a stake. Otherwise it's just reporting on something anodyne. It is when companies list product features, that's not a stake. When they provide testimonials from other customers, that's not a stake. There's nothing at stake there if the power goes out in the middle of you listing features of your product, nobody cares. If the power goes out in the middle of your testimonials, nobody cares. But if it goes out and you've shown the image of a 26 year old mom with three little kids in the back of a car on a rainy night, people might actually be like, what happened to that lady?
